patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 9-year-old girl. You seem anxious and answer vaguely unless asked. Start by saying: “My wrists and ankles have been hurting for months, and my hands and feet feel weaker and kind of changing shape.” Volunteer: aching/stiffness in both wrists and ankles (worse mornings and after activity), weakness with opening jars/turning keys/carrying bags, ankles feel like they might give out, swelling that comes and goes, and that people say you have a wide bridge to your nose.

If asked, add: it started when you were younger and slowly worsened; some fingers look shorter/pulled in; feet look flatter/wider and shoes fit differently; you have high blood pressure on medicine; some days puffy eyes/sock marks; foamy urine in mornings; fatigue.

Do not mention unless directly asked: protein in urine, decreased urination on swollen days, repeated minor “sprains,” reduced range of motion, family kidney/high-BP history.

Resist admitting: skipping BP pills due to dizziness, heavy OTC painkiller use, embarrassment about hands.

Misconceptions: say you googled rheumatoid arthritis/gout/carpal tunnel.

If clinician is empathetic, you soften, share more, and ask if it could be serious. Mention family worries about radiation, cost, genetic testing, insurance, and time off.

confounders: Juvenile idiopathic arthritis mimic (hand/wrist pain and functional limitation with normal inflammatory markers)

diagnosis.primary: multicentric carpo-tarsal osteolysis with or without nephropathy

correct_diagnosis.primary: Multicentric carpo-tarsal osteolysis (MCTO) with nephropathy (chronic kidney disease due to associated glomerulopathy)
acceptable_alternatives: Multicentric osteolysis, nodulosis, and arthropathy (MONA syndrome)
Juvenile idiopathic arthritis (mimic; inflammatory arthritis with wrist/ankle involvement)
Hereditary sensory and autonomic neuropathy with osteolysis (neuropathic osteolysis; mimic)
required_evidence: Clinical pattern of progressive osteolysis/shortening involving carpal and tarsal bones (typically childhood onset), often mistaken for inflammatory arthritis
Dysmorphic feature consistent with described syndrome spectrum (e.g., wide nasal bridge)
Renal involvement assessment: proteinuria on urine protein/creatinine ratio and/or reduced eGFR by Schwartz and/or elevated creatinine/BUN (to classify with vs without nephropathy)
Urinalysis evaluation for hematuria/casts to support glomerular disease when nephropathy present
Exclusion of primary inflammatory arthritis/infection as primary cause when osteolysis pattern and renal findings fit MCTO

expected_lab_results.Serum creatinine: May be elevated if nephropathy present; can be normal early in disease
expected_lab_results.BUN: May be elevated if nephropathy present; can be normal early in disease
expected_lab_results.eGFR (Schwartz): May be decreased (CKD) if nephropathy present; can be normal early
expected_lab_results.Urine protein/creatinine ratio: Often elevated (proteinuria) when nephropathy present; can be normal if no nephropathy
expected_lab_results.Urinalysis - blood: May be positive in glomerular disease; may be negative
expected_lab_results.Urinalysis - RBCs: May show microscopic hematuria when nephropathy present; may be absent
expected_lab_results.Urinalysis - casts: May show RBC casts or other casts if active glomerulonephritis; may be none
expected_lab_results.Serum albumin: May be low if significant proteinuria/nephrotic-range loss; may be normal if mild proteinuria/no nephropathy

medications: Lisinopril 0.07 mg/kg PO once daily (titrate as tolerated to reduce proteinuria; max 40 mg/day)
Acetaminophen 10-15 mg/kg PO every 6 hours as needed for pain (max 60-75 mg/kg/day; do not exceed 3,000 mg/day in adolescents/adults depending on local guidance)
non_pharmacological: Avoid NSAIDs due to CKD risk and patient intolerance/contraindication
Nephrology referral for CKD/proteinuria evaluation and longitudinal management; monitor for progression and complications
Orthopedics/hand-foot specialist referral for functional assessment, splinting/orthotics, and mobility support
Physical/occupational therapy for joint protection strategies, strengthening, and adaptive equipment
Genetics referral and counseling; consider testing for pathogenic variants associated with MCTO (e.g., MAFB) and family screening as appropriate

CKD education: maintain hydration, avoid nephrotoxins (including OTC NSAIDs), review all OTC/herbal supplements, and follow individualized dietary guidance if CKD confirmed (e.g., sodium moderation; protein intake per nephrology)
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Recheck kidney labs and urine protein/creatinine ratio in 2-4 weeks after starting ACE inhibitor (or sooner if symptomatic), then every 3 months if stable; earlier nephrology follow-up if eGFR reduced, rising creatinine, or significant proteinuria

must_include: Condition involves progressive bone loss in wrists/ankles (carpal/tarsal osteolysis) and can be associated with kidney disease
Kidney involvement is assessed by creatinine/eGFR and urine protein testing; results determine whether nephropathy is present
Why symptoms can mimic arthritis and why this diagnosis changes management (focus on supportive care and renal protection)
Pain control plan emphasizing acetaminophen and avoidance of NSAIDs
If proteinuria/CKD present: ACE inhibitor purpose (reduce protein in urine, protect kidney function) and need for monitoring kidney function after starting
Need for multidisciplinary care (nephrology, orthopedics, PT/OT, genetics)
Discuss prognosis variability: bone/joint function issues and possible kidney progression; importance of early renal monitoring
Review red flags requiring urgent care (decreased urine output, swelling, severe fatigue, shortness of breath, gross hematuria)
Confirm medication allergies and reinforce avoiding OTC NSAIDs
Avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, dehydration, unreviewed supplements); encourage medication reconciliation at every visit
Joint protection strategies, activity modification, and adherence to PT/OT and orthotic recommendations
Plan for scheduled lab monitoring and follow-up appointments

tool_call_sequence.description: Elicit presenting symptoms (wrist/ankle pain/swelling, deformity, function loss), onset/progression, prior 'arthritis' workup/treatments, family history, and screen for kidney symptoms and red flags.

Assess nephropathy and establish baseline renal status: BMP/CMP (creatinine), eGFR, urinalysis, urine protein quantification (UPCR or ACR), and blood pressure measurement (if represented as a lab/vital in the environment).
Interpret renal and urine results to determine presence/absence of nephropathy/proteinuria and guide safe medication choices.
Make/confirm diagnosis of multicentric carpo-tarsal osteolysis with or without nephropathy based on clinical pattern and renal assessment results; document whether nephropathy is present.
Explain diagnosis and why it can mimic arthritis; emphasize management focus (supportive joint care and renal protection), and outline multidisciplinary care needs.
Critical safety check before any prescribing; confirm medication allergies, including OTC analgesics and prior reactions.
Non-critical safety check before prescribing; review current meds/supplements for interactions (e.g., ACE inhibitor + potassium supplements, nephrotoxic agents).
Provide pain control plan emphasizing acetaminophen and avoidance of NSAIDs; if proteinuria/CKD present, initiate/plan ACE inhibitor therapy when appropriate with monitoring plan.
Explain treatment plan: acetaminophen use, NSAID avoidance, ACE inhibitor purpose and monitoring if indicated; reinforce red flags and medication reconciliation/avoid nephrotoxins.
Arrange follow-up for lab monitoring (renal function and urine protein), blood pressure review, symptom control, and referrals (nephrology, orthopedics, PT/OT, genetics).
Close encounter after confirming understanding, safety-netting, and follow-up plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome (RCVS)
acceptable_alternatives: Thunderclap headache due to reversible cerebral vasoconstriction
required_evidence: Recurrent thunderclap headaches peaking within 1 minute (often over days to weeks)
No evidence of aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage on initial evaluation (or only small convexity/cortical SAH)
Cerebral arterial vasoconstriction on CTA/MRA/DSA (multifocal segmental narrowing/"beading") OR high clinical suspicion with plan for repeat vascular imaging in 1–2 weeks if initial imaging normal
Reversibility of vasoconstriction within ~12 weeks (planned confirmation with follow-up imaging)
Assessment for and avoidance of common triggers (vasoactive drugs, postpartum state, exertion/sexual activity, cannabis/cocaine, serotonergic/sympathomimetic agents)

expected_lab_results.CBC - WBC: Typically normal (no leukocytosis suggestive of infection/vasculitis)

medications: Nimodipine 60 mg PO every 4 hours

non_pharmacological: Admit/observe with frequent neurologic checks if severe symptoms, focal deficits, seizure, or any hemorrhage/infarct suspected; otherwise close outpatient follow-up with strict return precautions
Discontinue/avoid potential triggers: triptans, ergotamines, decongestants/sympathomimetics, cocaine/amphetamines, cannabis, SSRIs/SNRIs if possible, high-dose caffeine/energy drinks
Avoid glucocorticoids (associated with worse outcomes in RCVS unless another diagnosis requires them)
Pain control with non-vasoconstrictive options; avoid triptans/ergots
Hydration and blood pressure management (avoid extremes; treat severe hypertension if present)
Plan repeat vascular imaging (CTA/MRA) in 1–2 weeks if initial study normal but suspicion remains; confirm resolution by ~8–12 weeks
Evaluate for complications (ischemic stroke, intracerebral hemorrhage, convexity SAH, PRES) and treat per standard protocols if present
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Neurology follow-up within 1 week; repeat vascular imaging in 1–2 weeks (or sooner if neurologic deficits) and again at 8–12 weeks to document reversibility.

must_include: RCVS involves temporary narrowing/spasm of brain arteries that typically resolves over weeks
Thunderclap headaches can be severe and require evaluation to rule out bleeding/aneurysm and stroke
Imaging may be normal early; repeat imaging is sometimes necessary to confirm the diagnosis and ensure resolution
Potential complications include stroke or brain hemorrhage; warning signs require urgent care
Calcium-channel blocker (e.g., nimodipine) is used to reduce vasoconstriction and headache burden; it is not an instant cure
Common side effects: low blood pressure, dizziness, flushing; advise to report syncope or worsening symptoms
Avoid steroids unless another condition is proven because they can worsen RCVS outcomes
Pain plan avoids vasoconstrictive headache medicines (no triptans/ergots)
Discuss and review all current medications/substances for triggers (including OTC decongestants, supplements, stimulants, cannabis, cocaine, serotonergic agents)
Set clear return precautions: new weakness/numbness, trouble speaking, vision loss, seizure, confusion, persistent/worsening thunderclap headache, fainting
Explain need for follow-up imaging and neurology follow-up to document reversibility and monitor complications
Avoid triggers and exertional/sexual activity that provokes thunderclap headaches until cleared by neurology
Avoid smoking/recreational drugs and limit caffeine/stimulants
Maintain hydration and sleep; manage stress

Required critical safety check before prescribing (e.g., calcium-channel blocker) and before suggesting OTC agents.

Required critical safety check to identify interactions with nimodipine/verapamil (e.g., CYP3A4 inhibitors, antihypertensives) and to screen for vasoactive triggers (triptans/ergots, decongestants, stimulants, serotonergic agents).

Required critical safety check before prescribing: hypotension, severe aortic stenosis/cardiogenic shock, significant bradycardia/heart block (agent-dependent), pregnancy/breastfeeding considerations, hepatic impairment, and clinical instability requiring ED care.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis, treatment expectations/side effects, trigger avoidance, return precautions, and why repeat imaging and neurology follow-up are needed.
Arrange neurology follow-up and repeat vascular imaging plan to document reversibility and monitor for complications; ensure clear timeframe and escalation plan.
Close after confirming understanding, safety netting, and follow-up plan.

Agent addresses concerns and includes all required elements: urgent warning signs (decreased urine, worsening lethargy/irritability, bleeding/bruising, severe headache/seizure, shortness of breath); sets expectations about hospital course and frequent monitoring; reviews allergies (amoxicillin-clavulanate and latex) and states how exposures will be avoided.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 29-year-old mom, cooperative and clear. Open with: “My baby suddenly started getting really stiff and his arms and legs keep twisting in weird ways.” Also volunteer that between spells he seems weaker/floppier, he’s fussier and cries during twisting, and this started after a bad cold/fever a couple weeks ago and hasn’t returned to normal.

If asked about episodes: mention eye-rolling or brief staring/unresponsiveness, startle-jerks with loud noises, clenched hands/pointed feet, broken sleep, and that he worsens after fevers. If asked about development/feeding/breathing: say he’s feeding less with coughing/choking, not sitting/reaching like before, and sometimes breathes fast when upset/sick.

Express worries: “Could this be meningitis?” and “Someone said cerebral palsy.” Ask about costs; assume genetics/specialists aren’t covered, therapy can be replaced by home exercises, and support services could risk job/immigration/insurance.

Withhold that you and your partner are cousins unless directly asked about family history/consanguinity; then admit it hesitantly and fear judgment. If the clinician is empathetic, relax, thank them, and share more details.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Infantile bilateral striatal necrosis
acceptable_alternatives: Leigh syndrome (subacute necrotizing encephalomyelopathy) with predominant basal ganglia involvement

required_evidence: Infant/young child with acute or subacute onset dystonia, parkinsonism, choreoathetosis, or developmental regression
Neuroimaging showing bilateral symmetric striatal (caudate/putamen) necrosis or T2/FLAIR hyperintensity consistent with bilateral basal ganglia injury
Autosomal recessive inheritance pattern (affected siblings/consanguinity) or confirmed biallelic pathogenic variant in a relevant gene
Exclusion of common acquired causes of bilateral basal ganglia injury (hypoxic-ischemic injury, carbon monoxide/cyanide exposure, severe hypoglycemia, CNS infection)

expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - Bicarbon: May be low if metabolic acidosis during decompensation (suggestive of systemic illness/mitochondrial crisis but nonspecific)
expected_lab_results.Comprehensive Metabolic Panel - BUN: Often normal; may be elevated with dehydration

Rehabilitation: PT/OT/speech therapy for motor and swallowing function; assess aspiration risk

Perform and document allergy check before prescribing; specifically confirm penicillin allergy details (urticarial rash) and any other medication reactions.
Screen for drug interactions with planned therapies (thiamine, biotin, levetiracetam, trihexyphenidyl, rescue meds) and any home meds/supplements.

Initiate empiric high-dose thiamine and biotin promptly; prescribe symptom-directed therapy as indicated (e.g., levetiracetam for seizures; trihexyphenidyl for dystonia) and supportive measures to reduce metabolic stress.

Verify contraindications before/while prescribing (critical): assess hepatic/renal status, swallowing/aspiration risk for oral meds, anticholinergic contraindications for trihexyphenidyl (e.g., urinary retention, glaucoma risk), and appropriateness of chosen doses/route for age/weight.

Complete communication milestones: explain diagnosis, explain treatment plan and urgency, address prognosis/concerns and red flags, and provide lifestyle/illness-plan guidance including avoidance of fasting and genetic counseling.
Arrange urgent neurology and metabolic/genetics follow-up, MRI review/ordering if not already done, therapy/rehab referrals, and monitoring plan for medication side effects.
Close encounter after ensuring safety-netting, follow-up, and documentation of allergies and plan.

correct_diagnosis.primary: PPoma (pancreatic polypeptide–secreting pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor)
acceptable_alternatives: Nonfunctional pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor with elevated pancreatic polypeptide

required_evidence: Markedly elevated fasting plasma pancreatic polypeptide (PP) above reference range, preferably confirmed on repeat fasting sample
Neuroendocrine tumor biochemical support (e.g., elevated chromogranin A) with other functional syndromes excluded by labs (normal/near-normal fasting gastrin, VIP, glucagon, fasting insulin)
Hyperglycemia may be present (elevated fasting plasma glucose and/or HbA1c) but is not required
No lab pattern consistent with alternative functional NETs (Zollinger-Ellison: high gastrin; VIPoma: high VIP; glucagonoma: high glucagon; insulinoma: inappropriately high insulin with low glucose)

expected_lab_results.Fasting plasma glucose: Often normal to elevated; may show impaired fasting glucose or diabetes (not diagnostic).
expected_lab_results.HbA1c: Normal to elevated (may reflect impaired glucose tolerance/diabetes).
expected_lab_results.Plasma pancreatic polypeptide (PP): Significantly elevated on fasting sample; ideally confirmed on repeat testing.
expected_lab_results.Chromogranin A: Often elevated (supportive of neuroendocrine tumor; interpret cautiously if confounders present).
expected_lab_results.Serum gastrin (fasting): Normal or not markedly elevated (helps exclude gastrinoma).
expected_lab_results.Fasting insulin: Not inappropriately elevated; expected to be appropriate for glucose level (helps exclude insulinoma).

medications: Octreotide LAR 20-30 mg IM every 4 weeks (or lanreotide 120 mg deep SC every 4 weeks) if symptomatic hormone-related effects or for tumor control in somatostatin-receptor–positive disease
Metformin 500 mg PO daily with food, titrate to 1000 mg PO twice daily as tolerated if diabetes/impaired glucose tolerance is present
non_pharmacological: Arrange confirmatory workup and staging: cross-sectional pancreatic imaging (contrast CT or MRI) and somatostatin receptor imaging (e.g., Ga-68 DOTATATE PET/CT) and tissue diagnosis when feasible
Refer to multidisciplinary team (endocrinology/medical oncology/surgical oncology) for resectability assessment; surgical resection if localized/resectable

If metastatic/unresectable: consider systemic options per staging and receptor status (e.g., PRRT with 177Lu-DOTATATE for SSTR-positive disease; everolimus or sunitinib for progressive disease) and liver-directed therapy when appropriate
Lifestyle counseling for glycemic control (dietary carbohydrate moderation, weight management, physical activity) and cardiovascular risk reduction
correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 4-6 weeks (earlier if symptomatic) to review repeat PP and other labs, obtain imaging results, and finalize staging and definitive management plan; then ongoing follow-up every 3-6 months based on disease status.

must_include: PPoma is a pancreatic neuroendocrine tumor that secretes pancreatic polypeptide; many cases have nonspecific or minimal hormone symptoms
Diagnosis is supported by markedly elevated fasting PP and neuroendocrine markers, with labs helping rule out other hormone-secreting tumors
Next steps require imaging (CT/MRI and somatostatin receptor PET) and often biopsy to confirm and stage
If localized, surgery offers the best chance of cure; treatment choice depends on stage and resectability
Somatostatin analogs can help control tumor growth and hormone-related symptoms in receptor-positive disease
If advanced disease, additional treatments may include PRRT or targeted therapies; plan is individualized in a multidisciplinary setting
Review penicillin allergy and ensure it is documented; clarify reaction history and avoid penicillin-class antibiotics unless formally evaluated
Discuss potential medication adverse effects (somatostatin analogs: GI effects, gallstones, glucose changes; metformin: GI effects, rare lactic acidosis risk in severe renal/hepatic disease)
Emphasize the importance of follow-up for staging and monitoring
If hyperglycemia is present: diet and activity measures to improve glucose control and reduce cardiovascular risk
Encourage adherence to scheduled imaging/lab monitoring and symptom tracking (new abdominal pain, weight loss, diarrhea, flushing, hypoglycemia symptoms)

Non-critical but expected before prescribing; review interactions for planned therapies (e.g., somatostatin analogs affecting glucose-lowering agents; other concurrent meds).

Close encounter after ensuring safety checks, education, and follow-up plan are completed.

correct_diagnosis.primary: Cryptogenic organizing pneumonia (COP)
acceptable_alternatives: Organizing pneumonia (secondary) pending exclusion of triggers
Nonresolving community-acquired pneumonia (to be ruled out)
required_evidence: Subacute onset (days–weeks) of cough and dyspnea with systemic symptoms and nonresolving course despite appropriate antibiotics
Imaging consistent with organizing pneumonia pattern (e.g., bilateral patchy peripheral/peribronchovascular consolidations and/or ground-glass opacities; migratory opacities)

Infectious workup not supportive of bacterial pneumonia (e.g., low procalcitonin; lack of leukocytosis) and no clear alternative cause identified (drug/toxin, connective tissue disease, aspiration, radiation)
Elevated inflammatory markers (CRP/ESR) may be present and support inflammatory process

expected_lab_results.CMP Potassium: Usually normal (monitor for hypokalemia with systemic corticosteroids)

non_pharmacological: Confirm diagnosis and exclude secondary causes (medication/toxin exposure review, autoimmune/CTD screening as indicated, consider bronchoscopy/BAL ± biopsy if diagnosis uncertain)

correct_treatment_plan.follow_up: Reassess in 1-2 weeks for symptom response and adverse effects; repeat chest imaging in 4-6 weeks; continue follow-up every 4-8 weeks during taper

Infection prevention while on steroids (hand hygiene, avoiding sick contacts, vaccination planning)

Initiate guideline-concordant ART promptly and prescribe indicated opportunistic infection prophylaxis when CD4 is low, selecting alternatives to TMP-SMX due to prior rash/pruritus and ensuring no contraindications/interactions.

Meet communication milestones: explain diagnosis (CD4/viral load meaning), treatment goals (viral suppression, CD4 recovery, U=U), side effects/urgent symptoms, adherence/support, safer sex/partner testing, vaccination and infection-prevention counseling, and importance of follow-up labs.

Arrange timely follow-up for ART tolerance/adherence and lab monitoring (viral load in ~2–4 weeks after initiation and then per guidelines; CD4 monitoring; prophylaxis reassessment), plus linkage to HIV care/support services.
Close encounter after ensuring safety checks completed, prescriptions placed appropriately, and follow-up plan and return precautions are provided.

Do not mention unless directly asked: brief dusky lips/breathing change during spells, morning arm jerks, worse with fever/missed sleep, snoring/pauses, high pain tolerance, low-sugar shakiness helped by juice, and coughing with thin liquids. Be resistant: admit skipping seizure meds due to sleepiness, struggling with insulin/checks, hiding aggression, and using a relative’s leftover calming med only if asked about meds/safety. patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, a 24-year-old anxious but clear speaker. Start by saying: “My skin has these thick, spiky patches that keep building up, especially on my hands and feet.” Volunteer that your palms/soles are very thick and hard, crack and hurt with walking or gripping, you’ve had it as long as you can remember, it slowly worsens/spreads, and regular lotions don’t help.

If asked, add rough patches on elbows/knees and sometimes arms/legs; tight/sore areas; cracks sting/bleed; worse in cold/dry weather; nails thicker/ridged; no blisters.

Withhold unless specifically asked about impact/behavior: embarrassment (avoid shaking hands), past red/tender cracks needing antibiotic cream, sensitivity when scrubbing, certain shoes worsen, and that you sometimes pick/cut it with a razor/clippers.

Express misconceptions: you worry it’s fungus or psoriasis; OTC “eczema” cream and a friend’s steroid didn’t help.

Show cost/job worries: assume prescriptions/derm/genetic testing won’t be covered; avoid appointments and underreport work impact.

If clinician is empathetic, you relax, admit embarrassment and picking, and ask if it’s contagious and what affordable options exist.
